title: Agentic Evals That Matter: Multi-Turn, Tool-Calling, and the Template Trap
description: The evals the team is missing and how to build them. This lesson moves from eval infrastructure to eval design: why a green single-turn score can ship a broken multi-turn agent, why the Gemma 4 launch tool-call pass rate of about ten percent was a chat-template formatting bug rather than a capability gap, how a seeded agent environment is scored with a state check, why held-out data and contamination discipline decide whether a score means anything, and a concrete context-disclosure privacy eval worth building. It closes the loop from why evals decide to which evals to build.
goals: Distinguish single-turn evaluation from multi-turn agentic tasks and explain why cross-turn state makes a green single-turn score hide a broken agent
Decompose tool-calling correctness into capability in the weights versus protocol in the chat template, and diagnose a low pass rate instead of assuming the model is weak
Explain the Gemma 4 launch template trap, where a formatting bug held the tool-call pass rate near ten percent while the weights were fine
Design an agentic eval with a seeded environment, a precise expected end state, and held-out data that guards against contamination
Describe a concrete context-disclosure privacy eval that scores whether an agent respects audience-specific disclosure boundaries

next_lesson_diagnostics.prompt: If the next lesson builds the context-disclosure privacy eval, what would you define first: the audiences and their allowed disclosure levels, the seeded scenario, the agent trigger, or the scoring rule that checks the boundary?
next_lesson_diagnostics.hint: Think about what must exist before an agent response can be judged as respecting or breaking a boundary.

next_lesson_diagnostics.prompt: When a new tool-calling eval reports a low pass rate, what would you inspect first to decide whether it is a template problem or a capability gap: the parsed tool-call format, the control tokens, the model reasoning, or the expected end state?
next_lesson_diagnostics.hint: One of these tells you whether the call could even be parsed before you judge whether it was correct.

next_lesson_diagnostics.prompt: To keep a new agentic eval trustworthy over time, what would you protect most carefully against contamination: the tool schemas, the seeded environments, the expected end states, or the held-out task set?
next_lesson_diagnostics.hint: Contamination inflates a score by letting the model memorize what it should have to generalize.

knowledge_graph_data.title: Agentic eval design in the subject map
knowledge_graph_data.description: This lesson teaches how to design the agentic evals the team is missing: single-turn versus multi-turn, the capability versus protocol template trap, seeded-environment scoring with held-out data, and a context-disclosure privacy eval. It builds on the eval infrastructure lesson and points toward quantization and release.

planning_rationale: Lesson 30 is the seq 6 advancement named in the subject 5 workpad: Agentic Evals That Matter. Lesson 29 taught the eval and serving infrastructure. This lesson deepens the eval-design half, which evals to build and why the ones the team has miss real failures. It teaches single-turn versus multi-turn evaluation, the capability versus protocol decomposition behind the Gemma 4 launch template trap where the tool-call pass rate sat near ten percent, how a seeded agent environment is scored with a state check, contamination and held-out discipline, and Frank's own context-disclosure privacy eval that Sara flagged as worth exploring. It is grounded in the 2026-07-09 Sara meeting plus public sources on tool-calling and multi-turn evaluation (Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard, tau-bench, Gemma prompt formatting, Hugging Face chat templates and tool use, FunctionGemma). The next lesson can build the privacy eval end to end.

source_context.author: cc-handauthored/agentic-evals-that-matter
source_context.derived_from: subject 5 workpad seq 6 (Agentic Evals That Matter) + 2026-07-09 Sara meeting

sources.title: Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3
sources.contribution: Multi-turn, multi-step tool-calling categories with backend state checks across turns, the basis for the single versus multi-turn contrast.

sources.title: FunctionGemma formatting and best practices
sources.contribution: Single-turn and parallel tool calls are supported while multi-turn and multi-step workflows are not, which is the exact gap the lesson teaches.

sources.title: Google AI Gemma 4 prompt formatting
sources.contribution: Roles, turn delimiters, thinking tokens, tool tokens, and tool responses are the runtime contract, which explains the template trap.

sources.title: Hugging Face Transformers chat templates
sources.contribution: Chat templates serialize messages into exact token sequences and wrong control tokens hurt behavior, the mechanism behind the ten percent pass rate.

sources.title: tau-bench paper and repository
sources.contribution: Final database-state comparison and reliability metrics across trials, the seeded-environment scoring and held-out discipline pattern.
